stakeholders.name: Iran/Revolutionary Guard

stakeholders.name: United States
stakeholders.role: Military aggressor and global energy market stabilizer
stakeholders.position: Launched weekend attacks on Iran, likely preparing naval response

stakeholders.name: Israel
stakeholders.position: Co-participant in weekend bombing campaign against Iran

stakeholders.role: World's largest oil importer and Iran trade partner
stakeholders.position: Calling for vessel protection and immediate ceasefire

stakeholders.name: Qatar
stakeholders.position: Victim of Iranian strikes, halted production at key facilities

stakeholders.name: Saudi Arabia
stakeholders.role: Regional oil power and Iranian rival
stakeholders.position: Under Iranian attack but maintaining limited damage narrative

stakeholders.name: Asian Import-Dependent Nations
stakeholders.position: Facing severe supply disruption (India, Thailand, Korea, Philippines)

stakeholders.name: Global Shipping Industry
stakeholders.position: 150+ tankers anchored, avoiding transit through strait

predictions.text: U.S. naval forces will deploy to escort tankers and attempt to reopen the strait
predictions.basis: Historical precedent from 1987-1988 Tanker War and strategic imperative to maintain global energy flows

predictions.text: Oil prices will exceed $100/barrel if closure persists beyond one week
predictions.basis: Already 12% weekly increase with 31% of seaborne crude flows disrupted

predictions.text: Iran will escalate attacks on regional energy infrastructure
predictions.basis: Pattern established with Qatar LNG and Saudi refinery strikes

predictions.text: China will increase diplomatic pressure for ceasefire while securing alternative supply routes
predictions.basis: China's position as largest oil importer and existing Iran trade relationship

predictions.text: Global recession triggered by sustained energy price shock
predictions.basis: 20% of global oil and LNG flows disrupted, with 60% gas price increases already recorded

historicalContext: This crisis echoes the 1987-1988 'Tanker War' during the Iran-Iraq conflict, when Iran similarly threatened Strait of Hormuz shipping, prompting Operation Earnest Will - the largest U.S. naval convoy operation since WWII. However, the current situation is unprecedented in scale, with Iran directly ordering complete closure rather than selective targeting. The strait has been a recurring flashpoint since the 1980s, with Iran repeatedly threatening closure during sanctions periods (2012, 2018-2019). The death of Ayatollah Khamenei, if confirmed, represents the most significant Iranian leadership decapitation since the 1979 revolution, potentially triggering succession crisis alongside the energy warfare.
implications: Global energy security architecture faces fundamental challenge as 20% of oil and LNG flows remain disrupted
Asian economies particularly vulnerable due to import dependence, with India facing 60% Middle East oil reliance
Potential acceleration of global energy transition as nations seek to reduce chokepoint vulnerabilities
International maritime law precedent being set for weaponization of critical trade routes
Global inflation pressures intensifying with 60% European gas price increases already recorded
Geopolitical realignment as China's energy security interests may conflict with U.S. military response
Regional energy infrastructure becoming primary warfare target, expanding conflict beyond traditional military assets
Global supply chain disruption extending beyond energy to affect all goods transiting the Persian Gulf
